title: Here’s the Truth About Whether Meta’s NameTag Face Recognition Tech ‘Exists’
gist: Meta built and deployed a technically functional (though inactive) face-recognition system called NameTag inside the Meta AI companion app for tens of millions of Ray-Ban glasses users starting around January 2026, then publicly denied the feature 'exists' after WIRED exposed it, while its on-device faceprint-matching architecture appears designed to skirt state biometric privacy laws like Illinois' BIPA.

themes.theme: Deployment scale understated by Meta's PR

insights.claim: WIRED's analysis found NameTag code present in the Meta AI companion app, downloaded tens of millions of times, as early as January 2026, with core functional components in place by May, contradicting Meta VP of Communications Andy Stone's public claim that the feature does not exist.
insights.speaker: Andrew Couts
insights.so_what: Signals a pattern of Meta downplaying or mischaracterizing shipped-but-inactive AI features to press and, by extension, regulators, relevant to assessing disclosure risk in Meta's AI product communications.
insights.quote: Here's a thing: Wired reports Meta didn't answer several questions about how this will work. How could we? The feature doesn't exist!
insights.locator: In response to our story, Andy Stone

insights.claim: An independent researcher, at WIRED's request, used the NameTag code within the Meta AI app to successfully recognize a photograph of philosopher Michel Foucault, demonstrating the face-recognition pipeline was technically operative rather than inert scaffolding.
insights.speaker: Andrew Couts
insights.so_what: Undercuts Meta's semantic defense; a working recognition pipeline dormant on millions of devices is a materially different risk profile than a feature that 'doesn't exist.'
insights.locator: a researcher who goes by the name Buchodi

insights.claim: Meta CTO Andrew Bosworth described NameTag in detailed, concrete terms on a podcast in early July 2026, weeks after Meta's official line was that the feature doesn't exist, describing it as identifying someone the glasses wearer had previously met.
insights.speaker: Andrew Couts
insights.so_what: Internal comms inconsistency between Meta's PR team and its CTO on a sensitive biometric product is a governance and IR red flag worth tracking ahead of any formal NameTag launch.
insights.quote: Somebody you met in person with your glasses on who introduced themselves—or you said, ‘OK, this is David, remember this person.’ ... That’s what we call a NameTags feature.
insights.locator: On the July 8 episode of The Most Interesting Thing in AI

insights.claim: After WIRED's June 4 report, Meta publicly called the story 'intellectually dishonest' and 'incredibly misleading' while simultaneously removing the NameTag code from the Meta AI app the very next day, an action inconsistent with a claim that the feature never existed.
insights.speaker: Andrew Couts
insights.so_what: The code-removal timing itself is evidence against Meta's 'doesn't exist' framing, a useful signal for evaluating the credibility of Meta's future statements on in-development AI features.
insights.quote: Meta removed the NameTag code from Meta AI the next day.

themes.theme: On-device architecture as a legal strategy

insights.claim: NameTag reportedly converts faces captured by Meta glasses into numerical 'faceprints' compared against a face database stored locally on the user's device (though populated by Meta's servers) rather than a single central database, an architecture that may let Meta argue compliance with state biometric laws like Illinois' BIPA and Texas' CUBI, which require explicit consent for face-recognition capture.
insights.speaker: Andrew Couts
insights.so_what: This on-device-versus-central-database distinction is the crux of ongoing biometric litigation and could determine whether Meta can legally ship NameTag in BIPA/CUBI states without new consent flows.
insights.locator: the NameTag system converts faces captured by Meta glasses

insights.claim: Case law on whether on-device biometric storage constitutes company 'possession' under BIPA is split: a 2021 federal ruling let a BIPA suit against Apple's Photos app proceed on an on-device possession theory (class-certified June 2026, pending rulings), while an Illinois appellate panel (2022) and a federal judge in a Samsung case (2024) found on-device-only storage does not constitute possession by the company.
insights.speaker: Andrew Couts
insights.so_what: NameTag's ultimate legal exposure hinges on unsettled case law about on-device biometric data possession, a live litigation-risk factor for Meta and any device maker doing on-device face matching.
insights.locator: In 2021, a federal judge let a BIPA class action

insights.claim: Meta previously abandoned automatic face recognition ('Tag Suggestions') on Facebook in 2019 after a $5 billion FTC privacy settlement and a $650 million Illinois state settlement specifically tied to that feature, giving CTO Andrew Bosworth direct institutional history with the legal cost of face-recognition missteps.
insights.speaker: Andrew Couts
insights.so_what: Quantifies Meta's roughly $5.65 billion combined tail-risk history on face recognition, a useful base rate for estimating potential liability if NameTag launches and draws similar scrutiny.

themes.theme: Key questions Meta still won't answer

insights.claim: Meta has declined to say whether NameTag would be opt-in, how faceprints and cropped images are retained, which third-party face-recognition software it licensed and when, or whether that licensing arrangement continues, precisely the questions courts have treated as decisive for legal liability.
insights.speaker: Andrew Couts
insights.so_what: The specific unanswered questions map directly onto the legal tests (optionality, ability to disable, company access to data) that will determine Meta's BIPA/CUBI exposure if NameTag ships.
insights.locator: When WIRED asked Meta in June
